serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw Brussels sprouts is approximately 1 cup, weighing about 88 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Brussels sprouts, raw — NDB No. 11098
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium) are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, which reports values consistent with USDA SR Legacy for raw Brussels sprouts. Extended micronutrient values (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamins, minerals, water) are taken from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170383), the closest approved-source exact match for raw Brussels sprouts. Both sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy. No adaptation or estimation was required for core macros.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw Brussels sprouts do not have a directly published GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked Brussels sprouts and comparable non-starchy cruciferous vegetables (e.g., cabbage, broccoli) listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. Non-starchy cruciferous vegetables consistently fall in the GI range of 10–20. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.95 × 0.88) / 100 = 1.18, rounded to 1.3 for a standard 88g cup serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (25mg/100g) and high potassium (389mg/100g) supporting DASH-diet targets
hypertension.guidance: Raw Brussels sprouts are highly suitable for hypertension management; their very low sodium content (25mg/100g) and high potassium (389mg/100g) support a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines, and dietary fibre (3.8g/100g) further contributes to arterial health — consume without added salt, butter, or high-sodium sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), high dietary fibre (3.8g/100g), and low sugar (2.2g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw Brussels sprouts have a low glycemic index (~15) and high dietary fibre (3.8g/100g), resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 1.3 per 88g serving, making them an excellent choice for glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes — avoid adding high-sugar glazes or sauces that would elevate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (0.3g/100g), high dietary fibre (3.8g/100g), and high antioxidant Vitamin C (85mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brussels sprouts support cardiovascular health through their very low fat content (0.3g/100g), cholesterol-free profile, high dietary fibre (3.8g/100g) which aids LDL reduction, and high Vitamin C (85mg/100g) providing antioxidant protection against oxidative vascular stress — best consumed without added saturated fats such as butter or bacon.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw Brussels sprouts (Brassica oleracea var. gemmifera) are small, compact, green, cabbage-like buds belonging to the cruciferous vegetable family. They have a firm, crisp texture and a mild, slightly bitter taste that becomes sweeter and nuttier when cooked. Originating in Belgium and widely cultivated across Europe and North America, they are commonly roasted, steamed, or stir-fried, but can also be eaten raw in salads when thinly sliced. Nutritionally, raw Brussels sprouts are an excellent source of Vitamin C (85mg/100g), dietary fibre (3.8g/100g), potassium (389mg/100g), and folate (61mcg DFE/100g). They are low in calories (43 kcal/100g), very low in fat (0.3g/100g), and low in sodium (25mg/100g). Their low glycemic index (~15) and high fibre content make them particularly beneficial for blood sugar management in Type 2 Diabetes. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports DASH-diet targets for hypertension. Brussels sprouts also contain sulforaphane, a bioactive glucosinolate-derived compound with documented anti-inflammatory and potential chemoprotective properties. They are naturally free of cholesterol, gluten, and dairy, making them suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and kosher diets.

are Brussels sprouts good for diabetes
how to eat raw Brussels sprouts
are Brussels sprouts good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Core macro nutrient data sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, an approved primary regional source, with values consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170383). Extended micronutrient data sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, an approved secondary global source. GI is an analogous estimate grounded in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed publication, which is the approved GI source under Project Veritas v2.
notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are from HPB Singapore Foodo. Extended micronutrient values (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170383) as these fields are not individually reported in the HPB source. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food that naturally contains no vitamin B12 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). Vitamin D is 0.0 as Brussels sprouts are a non-UV-exposed plant food with negligible vitamin D (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). Trans fat is 0.0 as Brussels sprouts are a raw plant food with no industrially produced trans fats (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food that naturally contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked Brussels sprouts and comparable cruciferous vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) AJCN peer-reviewed tables; no direct raw Brussels sprouts GI entry exists in the database. This is a generic raw ingredient record; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification applies, therefore no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.
